content.text: Extended Thinking
Extended Thinking
is a mode that gives Claude extra reasoning time before it answers, so it can work through harder problems more carefully instead of replying too quickly.[]
What it does

When extended thinking is on, Claude spends additional internal reasoning effort on the problem before producing its final response. That usually improves results on tasks that have many trade-offs, hidden dependencies, or multi-step logic.[]
When to use it
It is most useful for:
Architecture decisions
with multiple constraints.
Subtle bugs
that depend on state or edge cases.
Large refactors
spanning many files.
Code review
where non-obvious issues may be easy to miss.[]
It is less useful for simple, routine edits where speed matters more than deeper analysis.[]
How it fits with Plan Mode
Extended Thinking is about
how deeply Claude reasons
. Plan Mode is about
what Claude is allowed to do
They solve different problems and can be used together: Plan Mode keeps Claude read-only, while Extended Thinking helps it reason more thoroughly inside that safe boundary.[]
Practical effect

The trade-off is simple: better reasoning usually means more latency and more thinking tokens. For complex coding work, that is often worth it because it reduces back-and-forth and helps Claude stay on track.[]
Mental model
Think of it as asking Claude to
pause and think before speaking
. For easy questions, that pause is unnecessary. For difficult engineering work, it can make the difference between a shallow answer and a careful one.[]
Key takeaways
Extended Thinking adds more reasoning time before Claude პასუხs.[]
It is best for complex, multi-step, or ambiguous technical problems.[]
It costs more time and tokens than a normal response.[]
It complements Plan Mode, but it is not the same thing.[]
Best Practices
Following best practices ensures effective use of Extended Thinking in real-world applications.
Start with the simplest approach that solves your problem before adding complexity.
Test edge cases thoroughly, especially boundary conditions and error states.
Document your implementation decisions for future maintainability.
Monitor performance metrics and optimize based on actual usage patterns.
Follow security best practices and validate all inputs at system boundaries.
Use version control and code reviews for all production changes.
Establish clear error handling strategies before deployment.
Common Mistakes and How to Avoid Them
Understanding typical pitfalls in Extended Thinking helps you avoid costly errors.
Over-engineering solutions when simpler approaches would suffice — start minimal.
Neglecting error handling, leading to silent failures in production.
Skipping testing because the implementation appears straightforward.
Ignoring rate limits and quotas when designing at scale.
Not considering the user experience impact of latency or error states.

Security Considerations
Security is paramount when implementing Extended Thinking in production systems.
Never expose API keys in client-side code or public repositories.
Validate and sanitize all user inputs before processing.
Implement proper authentication and authorization controls.
Audit logs for all sensitive operations and data access.
Follow the principle of least privilege for all service accounts.
Practical Implementation Guide
A step-by-step approach to implementing Extended Thinking in your projects.
Assess your requirements: Define clear goals, constraints, and success criteria.
Design the architecture: Plan how components interact before writing code.
Implement incrementally: Build a minimal working version first, then iterate.
Test comprehensively: Cover both happy paths and error scenarios.
Deploy carefully: Use staging environments and gradual rollouts.
Monitor and iterate: Collect metrics and improve based on real-world usage.
Testing Strategies
Effective testing ensures your Extended Thinking implementation works correctly under all conditions.
Unit tests: Verify individual functions and components in isolation.
Integration tests: Confirm components work correctly together.
End-to-end tests: Validate complete user workflows from start to finish.
Performance tests: Ensure acceptable response times under load.
Chaos testing: Verify resilience by intentionally introducing failures.
Production Deployment Checklist
Configure proper logging and monitoring before going live.
Set up alerting for error rates, latency spikes, and cost anomalies.
Implement circuit breakers to prevent cascading failures.
Document runbooks for common operational scenarios.
Establish incident response procedures and escalation paths.
Schedule regular reviews of performance metrics and costs.
Advanced Patterns and Techniques
Advanced patterns help you get the most out of Extended Thinking for complex use cases.
Design for observability from the start — add tracing and metrics early.
Use feature flags to gradually roll out changes and minimize risk.
Implement graceful degradation so partial failures don't cause complete outages.
Consider multi-region deployments for high-availability requirements.
Document architecture decisions using Architecture Decision Records (ADRs).
Troubleshooting Guide
When issues arise, follow a systematic troubleshooting approach to identify and resolve them quickly.
Reproduce the issue: Identify the exact steps that trigger the problem.
Check logs: Review error messages and stack traces for clues.
Isolate variables: Change one thing at a time to identify the root cause.
Consult documentation: Verify your implementation matches the expected usage.
Seek community help: Share minimal reproduction cases when asking for help.

Integrating Extended Thinking with existing systems requires careful consideration of data flows and system boundaries.

Overview

Extended Thinking is a fundamental concept for anyone working with Claude and the Anthropic ecosystem. Understanding it deeply allows you to build more effective applications, write better prompts, and get more value from Claude's capabilities. This section provides a thorough exploration of the topic, covering both the theory and practical application aspects that you'll encounter in real-world usage.

Whether you're a beginner just starting out or an experienced developer looking to deepen your knowledge, this guide walks through everything you need to know about Extended Thinking. We'll cover the core concepts, walk through concrete examples, address common questions, and show you how to apply what you've learned in practical scenarios.
Why This Matters

Understanding Extended Thinking is important for several reasons. First, it forms the foundation for many other concepts in the Claude ecosystem. When you grasp this topic well, you'll find that more advanced subjects become significantly easier to understand and work with.

Second, practical knowledge of Extended Thinking directly improves the quality and efficiency of your work. Whether you're building AI-powered applications, crafting prompts for specific use cases, or integrating Claude into existing systems, this knowledge pays dividends in every interaction.
Saves time by avoiding common mistakes and misunderstandings
Enables more effective use of Claude's capabilities for your specific needs
Provides the foundation for understanding more advanced topics
Helps you make better architectural and design decisions
Reduces troubleshooting time when issues arise in production
Improves communication with teammates about AI integration strategies
Core Concepts
To fully understand Extended Thinking, it helps to break it down into its core components. Each component builds on the previous ones, creating a complete picture of how everything fits together.
Fundamental Principles

At its core, Extended Thinking is about how Claude processes and responds to different types of inputs and contexts. The fundamental principles that govern this behavior are consistent across all use cases, even though the specific implementation details may vary.
Consistency: Claude applies the same underlying principles regardless of context or phrasing
Context sensitivity: The same input can produce different optimal outputs depending on the surrounding context
Iterative refinement: Most effective usage involves refining your approach based on results
Transparency: Claude's behavior follows predictable patterns that you can learn and leverage
Key Components

The key components of Extended Thinking work together as an integrated system. Understanding each component individually is important, but understanding how they interact is what enables truly effective usage.
Input processing: How Claude receives and interprets your requests
Context management: How previous interactions influence current responses
Output generation: How Claude formulates and formats its responses
Quality control: Built-in mechanisms that ensure response quality and safety
Getting Started: Step-by-Step Guide
Here's a practical, step-by-step guide to getting started with Extended Thinking. Follow these steps in order to build a solid foundation.
Set up your environment: Ensure you have access to Claude through the appropriate interface for your use case (Claude.ai, API, or Claude Code).
Understand the basics: Read through this documentation thoroughly before attempting your first implementation.
Start simple: Begin with the simplest possible example to verify your understanding is correct.
Iterate and experiment: Modify your approach based on results, keeping notes on what works and what doesn't.
Review best practices: Consult the best practices section below to align your approach with proven patterns.
Test edge cases: Once your basic implementation works, test it with edge cases and unusual inputs.
Scale gradually: Only add complexity when your simpler version is working reliably.
Practical Examples
The best way to understand Extended Thinking is through concrete examples. These examples range from simple to complex, illustrating different aspects of the topic.
Basic Example
The following example demonstrates the most fundamental use case for Extended Thinking. This is a great starting point for understanding the core behavior.

# Example: Getting started with Extended Thinking
import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

# Basic usage example
message = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-opus-4-5",
    max_tokens=1024,
    messages=[
        {
            "role": "user",
            "content": "Explain Extended Thinking in simple terms with a practical example."
        }
    ]
)

print(message.content[0].text)
Advanced Example
This more advanced example shows how to use Extended Thinking in a real-world scenario with proper error handling, configuration, and best practices applied.

# Advanced example: Production-ready Extended Thinking implementation
import anthropic
from typing import Optional
import logging

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)

class ClaudeExtendedthinkingHandler:
    """Handles Extended Thinking with production best practices."""

    def __init__(self, api_key: Optional[str] = None):
        self.client = anthropic.Anthropic(api_key=api_key)
        self.model = "claude-opus-4-5"

    def process(self, input_text: str, max_tokens: int = 2048) -> str:
        """Process input with error handling and logging."""
        try:
            logger.info(f"Processing request of length {len(input_text)}")
            response = self.client.messages.create(
                model=self.model,
                max_tokens=max_tokens,
                messages=[{"role": "user", "content": input_text}]
            )
            result = response.content[0].text
            logger.info(f"Successfully generated response of length {len(result)}")
            return result
        except anthropic.RateLimitError:
            logger.warning("Rate limit reached, consider reducing request frequency")
            raise
        except anthropic.APIError as e:
            logger.error(f"API error: {e}")
            raise

# Usage
handler = ClaudeExtendedthinkingHandler()
result = handler.process("Your query here")
print(result)

Disadvantages

Frequently Asked Questions
These are the most common questions about Extended Thinking, based on developer feedback and support requests.
Q: What is the most important thing to understand about Extended Thinking?

A: The most important thing to understand is that Extended Thinking is a system that rewards careful, intentional usage. The more precisely you communicate your intent, the better results you'll get. Start with clear, specific inputs and refine based on the outputs you receive.
Q: How does this interact with other Claude features?

A: Extended Thinking works together with most other Claude features. It's particularly important to understand how it interacts with system prompts, conversation history, and any tools or integrations you're using. In most cases, these interactions are synergistic rather than conflicting.
Q: What are the performance implications?

A: Performance depends on several factors: the complexity of your inputs, the length of your context window, and the Claude model you're using. Generally, simpler and more focused inputs produce faster and more accurate results. For high-throughput use cases, consider batching and caching strategies.
Q: How do I debug issues?

A: When debugging Extended Thinking issues, start by isolating the problem. Simplify your input to the minimum that reproduces the issue, then gradually add complexity back. Check the API documentation for current behavior specifications and use logging to capture the full request-response cycle.
Best Practices
These best practices reflect lessons learned from production deployments and community experience with Extended Thinking.
Be specific and explicit about what you want — vague inputs produce vague outputs
Test with representative examples from your actual use case before scaling
Implement proper error handling for all edge cases and API errors
Use the appropriate Claude model for your use case and budget constraints
Monitor usage and costs from day one to avoid unexpected bills
Keep your prompts and configurations in version control
Document your implementation decisions for future team members
Build in feedback loops to continuously improve your implementation
Common Mistakes to Avoid
Assuming one approach works for all use cases — always test with your specific requirements
Not handling errors — API calls can fail for many reasons; always handle failures gracefully
Over-complicating prompts — simpler is usually better; add complexity only when needed
Ignoring token limits — always account for input and output token constraints in your design
Not testing edge cases — unusual inputs often reveal important behavior patterns
Skipping monitoring — without metrics, you can't improve what you can't measure
Summary

This guide covered Extended Thinking comprehensively: from core concepts and fundamental principles to practical examples, comparison of approaches, and best practices. The key insight is that effective usage comes from understanding the system deeply and applying that understanding consistently. Start simple, test thoroughly, and iterate based on results.
How Claude Code Uses Extended Thinking Internally

Claude Code automatically activates extended thinking when it detects that a task exceeds a certain complexity threshold. This activation is not something you configure explicitly for every request — the model evaluates the incoming prompt and decides internally whether spending additional reasoning time will materially improve the quality of its output. For routine edits, such as fixing a typo or adding a missing import, extended thinking is skipped entirely because the answer is immediate and unambiguous. For harder tasks — refactoring an authentication module, designing a caching strategy, or investigating a flaky test — the model enters a structured internal deliberation phase before producing the final response.

During the extended thinking phase, Claude Code builds a working mental model of the problem. It identifies constraints, considers multiple solution paths, evaluates trade-offs between them, checks for edge cases, and only then commits to a plan. This process is invisible to you unless you are running in interactive mode, where a collapsible Thinking block appears before the final answer. The key insight is that the thinking phase reduces the probability of producing a superficially plausible but ultimately incorrect response — a failure mode that is common in complex codebases where many pieces of state interact with each other.

Extended thinking is most impactful on tasks that have a large solution search space. When Claude Code is asked to refactor a large codebase, there are dozens of valid approaches, each with different implications for performance, maintainability, and backward compatibility. Without thinking, the model tends to pick the first plausible approach it encounters. With thinking, it can systematically explore the alternatives, reject the ones that introduce regressions, and arrive at an approach that better balances all the competing requirements. This distinction becomes especially visible when the task involves cross-cutting concerns — changes that ripple across many files simultaneously.

You can also influence when extended thinking activates by framing your prompts appropriately. Providing detailed context about constraints, listing requirements explicitly, and asking for a plan before implementation all signal to the model that the task warrants careful deliberation. Conversely, for quick queries where latency matters, keeping prompts short and specific tells the model that speed is the priority. Understanding this dynamic lets you get the best of both worlds: fast responses for routine work and thorough analysis for complex work.

graph TD
    A[User Request] --> B{Task Complexity}
    B -->|Simple| C[Direct Response]
    B -->|Complex| D[Extended Thinking Phase]
    D --> E[Internal Reasoning]
    E --> F[Synthesized Response]
    C --> G[Output]
    F --> G
    style D fill:#1e40af,color:#fff
    style E fill:#2563eb,color:#fff
    style G fill:#166534,color:#fff
The --think Flag in the CLI

When you launch Claude Code from the command line, the --think flag forces extended thinking mode for the entire session, regardless of whether the model would have activated it automatically. This is useful in two scenarios: when you know the task is complex and want to guarantee thorough reasoning upfront, and when you want to experiment with extended thinking on a task that might be borderline complex to see whether it produces a meaningfully better output.

The flag works at the session level, not the individual message level. Once you pass --think when starting claude, every prompt in that session benefits from extended thinking. This is intentional — complex engineering sessions rarely consist of a single hard question followed by trivial follow-ups. More often, an initial complex question spawns a series of follow-up questions that are equally complex, so keeping thinking active throughout the session is the right default behavior.

You can combine --think with other CLI flags for more precise control. The --max-turns flag limits the number of agentic iterations, which is important when you are using thinking for long-horizon tasks like autonomous codebase refactoring. Without a turn limit, a thinking-enabled session might spend a large number of tokens exploring a deeply nested problem space. Setting a reasonable limit ensures the session stays focused while still benefiting from extended deliberation on each individual step.

The --think flag does not change the model being used — you are still sending requests to the same claude model configured in your settings. What changes is the inference parameter that tells the model to allocate additional compute to the reasoning phase before generating a response. The practical effect is that you will notice slightly longer latency before the first token of the response appears, followed by a higher-quality answer that has accounted for more of the problem's complexity.

# Force extended thinking for a session
claude --think "Refactor the authentication module to use JWT tokens"

# Combine thinking with a turn limit for complex autonomous tasks
claude --think --max-turns 10 "Design a microservices architecture for our e-commerce platform"

# Debug a tricky async race condition
claude --think "Why is this race condition happening in our async queue processor?"

# Architecture review with thinking
claude --think "Review the current database schema and suggest normalization improvements"

# Security audit
claude --think "Audit the API endpoints in src/routes for injection vulnerabilities"

# Migration plan generation
claude --think "Create a migration plan from callbacks to async/await across the entire codebase"

# Performance investigation
claude --think "Why does the batch import job slow down after processing 10,000 records?"

# Dependency audit
claude --think "Review all npm dependencies and flag those with known security issues or outdated majors"
Specific scenarios where --think delivers the most value:
Cross-module refactors
— when a change must propagate consistently across ten or more files with different conventions and calling patterns.
Database schema migrations
— when you need to reason about backward compatibility, index performance, and data integrity simultaneously.
Security audits
— when you need the model to trace data flows from input to output to identify injection or privilege escalation risks.
Concurrency bug investigation
— when timing-dependent bugs require reasoning about thread interleaving and shared mutable state.
API contract design
— when you need to evaluate multiple REST, GraphQL, or RPC designs against a set of evolving client requirements.
Performance optimization
— when identifying the true bottleneck requires understanding the full call chain rather than just the apparent hot path.
Dependency upgrade planning
— when a major version bump introduces breaking changes that require coordinated updates across transitive dependencies.
Test suite design
— when you need to design a test architecture that covers edge cases systematically rather than reactively.
